edibility: Technically edible but generally regarded inedible due to its gelatinous, rubbery texture and complete lack of flavor. Contains no poisonous compounds but offers no culinary value.
taste: Bland and jelly-like with no distinctive flavor. Takes on the taste of any broth or liquid it is cooked in, but remains texturally unpleasant.
search: Found in autumn and winter on dead or dying oak branches, particularly after rain when the gelatinous fruiting bodies swell and become visible. Identified by its black, translucent, brain-like or ear-like gelatinous appearance. Look-alikes include Exidia nigricans (similar black jelly fungus) and Auricularia species (wood ear), though careful examination of branching pattern and texture helps distinguish them.
